era_context: The First Roman Civil War is in full swing. Sulla's forces are fighting the Marian faction (now led by Cinna and his allies) across Italy. The conflict has devastated the peninsula, with armies recruiting heavily from Italian allies and mercenaries. Mithridates VI remains a threat in the East, though temporarily contained by the Treaty of Dardanus. The Republic's institutions are paralyzed; the Senate is divided, and the consulship is contested. This year marks the climactic phase of the civil war, culminating in Sulla's decisive victory.

events.title: Battle of the Colline Gate: Sulla's Decisive Victory in the Civil War
events.description: Lucius Cornelius Sulla defeats the Marian forces under Gaius Marius the Younger (son of the famous general) and Cinna at the Battle of the Colline Gate, just outside Rome. The battle is fierce and nearly goes against Sulla; his right wing is nearly broken, but his left wing, under Lucretius Afella, rallies and turns the tide. The Marian forces are routed, and Marius the Younger is killed. This victory gives Sulla undisputed control of Rome and Italy. The battle marks the effective end of the First Civil War, though mopping-up operations continue. Sulla's triumph establishes him as the dominant political figure in Rome and sets the stage for his dictatorship and the proscription lists that will follow.
key_figures: Cinna (consul, Marian leader, killed before the battle)

sources.name: Livy, History of Rome (fragmentary, known through epitomes)

events.certainty_note: The battle is well-attested in multiple sources. The date is traditionally given as 82 BCE, but some sources place it in 83 BCE. Modern scholarship generally favors 82 BCE, but the uncertainty of ±1 year is inherent in ancient chronology for this period.

events.title: Death of Lucius Cornelius Cinna
events.description: Lucius Cornelius Cinna, the consul and leader of the Marian faction, is killed during the civil war. Cinna had been a key figure in the Marian resistance to Sulla, but he is murdered by his own troops (or by political enemies) before the final battle at the Colline Gate. His death removes the most prominent Marian leader and leaves the faction leaderless at a critical moment. Cinna's removal from the political scene is significant because he had represented a more moderate faction within the Marian coalition; his death leaves the field to more radical elements and to Sulla's uncompromising victory.
key_figures: Lucius Cornelius Cinna (consul and Marian leader)
events.certainty_note: Cinna's death is well-attested, though the exact circumstances and date are somewhat unclear. Ancient sources agree he died during the civil war, likely in 84 or 83 BCE.
cross_references: Related to -83-001 (Battle of the Colline Gate)

events.title: Sertorius Leads Lusitanian Revolt Against Sulla
events.description: Quintus Sertorius, a Roman general and supporter of the Marian faction, flees Italy and establishes himself in Lusitania (modern Portugal and western Spain) as a rival to Sulla's authority. Sertorius had been proscribed (marked for death) by Sulla and seeks to build a power base in the western provinces. He allies with local Lusitanian tribes and begins a prolonged guerrilla war against Roman forces loyal to Sulla. This revolt, which will last until 72 BCE, represents the first serious challenge to Sulla's authority outside Italy and demonstrates the fragility of Sulla's control over the provinces. Sertorius's rebellion is significant because it shows that the civil war is not truly over and that Sulla's enemies remain dangerous.
key_figures: Quintus Sertorius (Roman general and Marian supporter)
events.certainty_note: Sertorius's flight to Hispania is well-attested, but the exact date is uncertain. Ancient sources place it during the civil war period (84–82 BCE), with 83 BCE being a reasonable estimate. The beginning of his revolt is sometimes dated to 80 BCE.
cross_references: Related to -84-002 (Sulla's march on Rome and proscriptions)
Leads to prolonged conflict: -72-001 (End of Sertorian War)

disconfirming_evidence: The date of the Battle of the Colline Gate is disputed: most modern scholars favor 82 BCE rather than 83 BCE. The battle's significance as a 'decisive' victory is sometimes overstated; Sulla still faced significant opposition in the provinces (Sertorius, Lepidus) and had to consolidate power through proscriptions and constitutional reform. The narrative of Sulla's 'triumph' obscures the fact that the civil war created deep scars in Roman society and that Sulla's subsequent dictatorship was widely resented.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 83 BCE is rich but entirely dependent on sources written 100+ years later. No contemporary primary texts survive. The sources are heavily influenced by pro-Sullan bias (Livy, Appian) or pro-Marian bias (some Greek sources). The perspective is overwhelmingly elite and Roman; the impact on Italian allies, slaves, and non-elite populations is largely invisible. The narrative is heavily shaped by later historians' views on the causes of the Republic's fall. Eastern and provincial sources are sparse.

graph_edges.note: Sulla's victory at the Colline Gate enabled his dictatorship and proscriptions
Cinna's death left the Marian faction leaderless before the final battle
Sertorius's revolt in Hispania initiated a decade-long conflict
